celebration: THE MIRROR LEAF — no new sound this week, but the hardest week of all for a Mandarin-first ear. The class earns the Mirror Leaf (ship | sheep etched face to face) for hearing the difference between a short vowel and a long one. This week exists FOR them.

songs.title: Ship or Sheep?
songs.lyrics:
[Intro]
(ih… ee… ih… ee…)
Short… and long…

[Hook]
Ship or sheep? (which one?)
Ship or sheep? (listen!)
ih — ship! ee — sheep!
Say it slow! (ship… sheep…)

[Verse 2]
Hit or heat? (ih — ee!)
Fit or feet? (ih — ee!)
Sit or seat? (ih — ee!)
One is short, one is long!

[Verse 3]
Pin or pen? (ih — eh!)
Hat or hut? (a — uh!)
Cat or cut? (a — uh!)
Bag or bug? (a — uh!)

[Bridge]
A potato?! (NO!)
P-p-potato! Ship? Sheep? NO!
(ih… ee…)

[Outro]
Ship… sheep… (say it slow…)
Which one? (BOTH!)
(ih… ee… shhh…)
songs.sunoStyle: dark trap, 68 bpm, heavy 808 bass, sparse hi-hats, deep whisper-rap verses, kids choir chant on hook, playful spooky, minimal, clean vocals, nursery trap
songs.sunoNotes: 🔁 THE MINIMAL-PAIR ANTHEM — the EAL week's whole reason. Contrast is the beat: the short lax vowel /ɪ ɛ ʌ/ is a quick clipped hit, the long tense vowel /iː/ is HELD. 'ih' vs 'ee' sung as pure vowels (phonetic respelling — printed ship/sheep). Mandarin has no tense/lax split, so exaggerate the length difference hard. Whole words only — never spell-split ship or sheep.

songs.title: Ship or Sheep?
songs.lyrics:
[Intro]
(We can hear it. We can hear it!)

[Hook]
The sheep is on the ship!
The sheep is on the ship!
Ship? Or sheep? (which? which?)
Both! (the sheep AND the ship!)

[Verse 2]
The sheep sees the sea.
The ship is on the sea.
The sheep sits on a seat.
(safe, sheep, safe!)

[Verse 3]
The wind! The ship can tip!
Hold on, sheep! (hold on!)
Ship? Or sheep? (both! both!)
The sheep is safe.

[Outro]
Not a ship… not a sheep… (shhh…)
What is it?!
(POTATO!)
Ship or sheep? (both!)
songs.sunoStyle: dark trap, 68 bpm, heavy 808 bass, sparse hi-hats, deep whisper-rap verses, kids choir chant on hook, playful spooky, minimal, clean vocals, nursery trap
songs.sunoNotes: Frame: 'Ship or sheep?' — the story dramatizes the minimal pair, Sheep afloat on a ship. Every line makes the /ɪ/ vs /iː/ choice matter (a sheep on a seat, not a sit). The 'both!' answer is the joke: the child who can hear both is the child who wins. Whisper the outro; the potato is neither ship nor sheep.

spreads.artNote: A little wooden ship on a dark glassy sea. The old friend from Week 27, back for the review.
Sheep — the fluffy round white sheep with kind dark eyes — standing in the spotlight. This week the class can SPELL that word: sh-ee-p.
Sheep on the shore gazing out at the little ship. The two words, side by side, the whole book's game.

spreads.text: The sheep is on the ship!
spreads.artNote: Sheep clambered aboard, delighted, a bit wobbly.
A split spread: the ship on one side, Sheep on the other, a big question mark between. The minimal pair made visible.

spreads.text: The ship is on the sea. The sheep is on the ship.
spreads.artNote: Wide shot — Sheep aboard the ship, the ship far out on the moonlit sea. Everything nested and calm.

spreads.text: The wind! The ship can tip!
spreads.artNote: A gust — the ship leaning hard, Sheep sliding, wide-eyed. Suspense.

spreads.text: The sheep sits on a seat.
spreads.artNote: Sheep safely wedged onto a little bench seat, hooves gripping. 'Sits' and 'seat' in one sentence — the pair, resolved. Relief.

spreads.text: Not a ship. Not a sheep!
spreads.artNote: Between the ship's rail and the sheep sits a single potato in a tiny life-vest. No noun printed — the class SHOUTS it. Sheep and the ship both stare.

sentences: The sheep is on the ship.
The sheep sits on a seat.
The ship is on the sea.
extras: THE MINIMAL-PAIR SORT (the week's whole point): pairs printed on the SAME card, face to face — ship/sheep, hit/heat, fit/feet, bit/beat, sit/seat, pin/pen, hat/hut, cat/cut, bag/bug. Teacher says ONE; the child touches which. Then the child says one; the teacher touches. Short vowel = quick, long vowel = held. 🚨 Mandarin has no tense/lax vowel distinction — this drill is not enrichment, it is the missing brick.
MIRROR LEAF ceremony: the class hangs a special leaf on the Pattern Tree with ship | sheep mirrored across a silver line — the only leaf that celebrates HEARING rather than a new letter.
The 'listening ladder': line the pairs up short-to-long and walk the vowel up (ih → ee), a body sound-gradient. Feel the mouth stretch.
No new words this week by design — every drill word is stock the class already owns. The work is the EAR, not the eye.

teacherFocus: 🚨 THE EAL WEEK — the hardest of the whole program for a Mandarin-first ear, and the reason it has a week of its own. No new sound: instead, the ear. Mandarin makes no tense/lax vowel distinction, so ship and sheep sound identical to a child who has never had to hear the difference. Run the minimal-pair sort all week — same card, two words face to face — and make the length audible: short vowel is a quick clip, long vowel is HELD. Teacher-say-child-touch, then child-say-teacher-touch. Hang the Mirror Leaf: the one leaf that celebrates a trained ear, not a new letter.
